questions.question: You're building a customer support bot and want consistent, predictable responses. Which temperature setting makes the most sense?
options: High temperature, so the bot can get creative with its answers
Low temperature, to reduce randomness and keep responses stable
Temperature doesn't affect customer support bots, only creative tools
Maximum temperature, so no two responses are ever the same
questions.explanation: Low temperature makes the model sample from a narrower range of likely tokens, producing more consistent and predictable responses. High temperature is better for creative tasks where variety is desirable.

questions.question: A language model is generating a response token by token. Which statement is true?
options: Each token is chosen independently with no influence from previous tokens
The entire response is generated at once and then broken into tokens
Each token is chosen based on all the tokens that came before it
Tokens are retrieved from a database of pre-approved responses
questions.explanation: This is the core mechanism: each token is predicted based on all previous tokens in the sequence. The response builds up word by word, each choice informed by everything that came before.

questions.question: Karel is a fraud reporting agent. A colleague suggests setting Karel's temperature to 1.5 to make him 'more natural and conversational'. What is the main risk?
options: Karel will respond too slowly due to higher computational load
Karel may produce inconsistent or unpredictable responses to distressed customers
Karel will refuse to answer questions outside his training data
Karel will start using informal language that confuses the bank's systems
questions.explanation: High temperature introduces sampling randomness, the same question may get very different responses on different runs. For a fraud agent handling distressed customers, you need calm, consistent, predictable language every time. Low temperature is the right call.
